title: Century: Spice Road
publisher: Plan B Games / Next Move Games

subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content:
Core game components: 36 Point Cards, 53 Merchant Cards, 105 wooden Spice Cubes in 4 colors (yellow turmeric, red saffron, green cardamom, brown cinnamon), 4 plastic Spice Bowls, 20 Silver Coins, 10 Gold Coins, and 5 Caravan Cards.

How these components function in play:
- Merchant Cards build your action engine (production, conversion, upgrading).
- Point Cards are your primary victory point source and are claimed by paying spice combinations.
- Spice Cubes are your economy and are upgraded from low value to high value (yellow -> red -> green -> brown).
- Coins are bonus points earned by claiming point cards quickly.
- Caravan Cards indicate each player’s personal spice capacity limit and serve as their player aid area.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
Each player takes 1 Caravan Card and places it face up in front of them.

Each player begins with exactly 2 starting Merchant Cards in hand:
1) A Production card that gives 2 yellow cubes.
2) An Upgrade card that lets you upgrade cubes by a total of 2 steps (for example, two yellows to two reds, or one yellow to one green across two steps).

Give starting spices by turn order:
- 1st player: 3 yellow
- 2nd player: 4 yellow
- 3rd player: 4 yellow
- 4th player: 3 yellow + 1 red
- 5th player: 3 yellow + 1 red

All players keep spices in front of them as their caravan inventory. Your practical capacity is 10 cubes unless otherwise modified by scenario/variant. If you ever exceed capacity, discard down immediately.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content:
1) Shuffle the Merchant deck and reveal 6 Merchant Cards in a row (left to right).
2) Shuffle the Point deck and reveal 5 Point Cards in a row.
3) Place 1 Gold Coin on the leftmost Point Card and 1 Silver Coin on the second Point Card (if coin supply and setup conditions allow).
4) Place remaining coins near the play area as a general supply.
5) Randomly determine first player; play proceeds clockwise.

Important pre-turn state:
- All players start with only their 2 basic cards in hand.
- No cards are in any player discard area at start.
- Open information rows (merchant and point) are visible to all players before the first turn begins.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
On your turn, perform exactly ONE of these 4 actions:
1. Play a Merchant Card from your hand.
2. Acquire a Merchant Card from the market row.
3. Claim a Point Card from the point row.
4. Rest (recover all previously played Merchant Cards back into your hand).

After your one action resolves fully, your turn ends immediately. There are no bonus actions unless explicitly granted by a card effect. Then play passes clockwise.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
Action 1 - Play a Merchant Card
- Choose 1 card from your hand and place it face up in your played area.
- Resolve its effect exactly once, unless the card text explicitly allows repetition.

Merchant card effect types:
A) Production cards: Gain the cubes shown.
B) Trade cards: Exchange input cubes for output cubes shown on the card. Most trade cards can be repeated multiple times in one play as long as you can keep paying the full input each repetition.
C) Upgrade cards: Increase cube value along the spice chain (yellow -> red -> green -> brown) by the number of upgrade steps shown. You may split steps among different cubes.

Capacity rule during card resolution:
- If resolving a card would leave you above caravan capacity, you must discard excess cubes immediately after resolution.

Action 2 - Acquire a Merchant Card
- Take 1 card from the 6-card merchant row.
- Cost to acquire depends on position:
  - Leftmost card: free
  - Any card to the right: place 1 cube from your caravan onto each card to its left that you skip over.
- You must pay all skipped positions to take the target card.
- If the acquired card has cubes already on it, take those cubes into your caravan immediately.
- Put your newly acquired card into your hand (ready to be used on a later turn).
- Slide cards left to fill the gap, then refill the rightmost slot from deck.

Action 3 - Claim a Point Card
- Choose 1 face-up point card.
- Pay exactly the spice combination shown by discarding required cubes to general supply.
- Take that point card into your score area.
- Also take any coin currently on that card (gold/silver) as bonus points.
- Refill the point row from deck so 5 cards remain visible, if possible.

Action 4 - Rest
- Take all Merchant Cards from your played area back into your hand.
- You do not gain cubes or points from resting; this is purely card recovery.

Frequently checked legality notes:
- You can only play cards that are in your hand.
- You cannot partially claim a point card; full payment is required.
- You can claim at most one point card per turn (because each turn is one action).
- You may choose any legal action regardless of what action you took on your previous turn.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
Open Market Pressure (Merchant Row Tax):
When you buy deeper cards, the cubes you drop onto earlier cards create opportunities for opponents. This is a deliberate tempo tradeoff: stronger card now versus free resources you hand to others.

Engine Compression via Rest Timing:
Because played cards are unavailable until you Rest, sequencing matters. Strong turns often come from delaying Rest until your hand is exhausted, then replaying your best card chain immediately next cycle.

Spice Value Ladder:
Spice quality increases strictly in one direction (yellow -> red -> green -> brown). Most high-value point cards require upper-tier cubes, so early upgrade infrastructure is usually essential.

Coin Race Incentive:
Gold/Silver coins reward early claims on the left side of point row. Fast claim tempo can create an immediate scoring cushion even before your full engine matures.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
Endgame trigger:
- The game ends when one player has claimed the required number of Point Cards for the player count (commonly 6 cards in 2-3 player games, 5 cards in 4-5 player games).
- Finish the round so each player has taken the same number of turns.

Final scoring sources (ALL point sources):
1) Points printed on your claimed Point Cards.
2) Bonus points from collected coins:
   - Gold Coin = 3 VP
   - Silver Coin = 1 VP
3) Remaining spice cubes converted to endgame points using the standard leftover conversion rule (higher-tier cubes are worth more).

Winner:
- Highest total VP wins.

Tiebreakers:
1) Tied player with more remaining spice cubes wins.
2) If still tied, tied players share victory.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content:
1) Build one reliable conversion lane immediately.
In your first few turns, prioritize at least one card that either upgrades efficiently or converts low spices into mid/high spices. Without this, you will stall before first major point claims.

2) Avoid overpaying for deep-row merchant cards early.
Paying 2-4 cubes in tax can erase your entire tempo in a gateway game this tight. Do it only for cards that are true engine anchors.

3) Claim your first point card sooner than feels "perfect."
Many new players wait too long trying to assemble a huge turn. A medium-value early claim plus coin bonus often outpaces a delayed big claim.

4) Use rest turns as planned pivots, not emergency brakes.
Count how many cards remain in hand and schedule your rest so your next turn can immediately execute a full-value card sequence.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
1) Hoarding yellow cubes too long.
Low-tier cubes clog capacity and reduce flexibility. Convert upward early so you are not forced to discard valuable potential.

2) Buying "interesting" cards without engine fit.
A card can be strong in isolation but weak in your current deck flow. If it does not improve your next 2-3 turns, skip it.

3) Resting too often.
Frequent rests mean fewer productive turns. Try to extract full value from hand before recovering cards.

4) Ignoring opponent coin pressure.
If others are close to claiming point cards, delaying can cost both card value and coin bonuses. Track what opponents can pay now, not just what they might pay later.

5) Chasing only top-value point cards.
High-value cards are efficient, but tunnel vision causes dead turns. A sequence of smaller, fast claims can win by tempo and coin pickups.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
Decision 1: Engine growth vs immediate scoring
- Engine growth: stronger late turns, but risk falling behind in claim race.
- Immediate scoring: secures coins and pace, but may cap long-term efficiency.

Decision 2: Deep-row card purchase vs resource conservation
- Deep-row purchase can unlock premium effects.
- Conserving cubes preserves your next claim timing and avoids helping opponents with dropped cubes.

Decision 3: Rest now vs squeeze one more action
- Rest now if your next hand cycle creates a high-value chain.
- Delay rest if you still have any card that advances either your next claim or your spice quality curve this turn.

metadata.notes: Primary PDF text extraction failed in provided source package; content compiled from official publisher rules references and standard Century: Spice Road rules framework.
